                  BEFORE THE GOVERNING BOARD OF THE
                FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                     County of Sacramento, State of California

  AMENDMENT OF THE FISCAL YEAR 2024-2025 BUDGET OF THE GENERAL
 FUND (341A) TO REFLECT THE TRANSFER OF BUDGET FUNDS WITHIN THE
GENERAL FUND (341A) FROM THE UNRESERVED FUND BALANCE (74000000)
  TO THE BOND FUND (341C) TO REIMBURSE THE BOND FUND FOR COSTS
                    RELATED TO JIM STRENG PARK

WHEREAS, The Board of Directors for the Fair Oaks Recreation and Park District
passed Resolution No.052125-01, authorizing the transfer of Unreserved Fund Balance
monies encumbered in the 2024-2025 Budget in the General Fund (341A) for
reimbursement to the Bond Fund (341C) for costs related to the construction of Jim
Streng Park;

WHEREAS, the procedure to transfer and designate these funds requires appropriate
budget amendment action.

THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED that the Fair Oaks Recreation and Park
District Board of Directors approve the transfers as follows:

A. Increase the 341A Operating Transfer Out (5059800) by $517,914;
B. Decrease the 341A Unreserved Fund Balance (74000000) by $517,914.
C. Increase the 341C Operating Transfer In (59599100) by $517,914;
D. Decrease the 341C Miscellaneous Other Revenue (97979000) by $517,914.

Dear Board of Directors:
The Bond Oversight Committee certifies that the Fair Oaks Recreation and Parks District is in
compliance with the requirements of Article XIIIA, Section 1(b)(3) of the California Constitution.
Attached you will find a summary of the vendors and expenditures the committee selected at
random to review, as well as the complete list of expenditures we were provided.
You will also find a summary of the committee’s proceedings and activities for the preceding
year in the form of meeting minutes. The Committee has met the obligation of its bylaws to meet
at least four (4) times annually in 2024 by meeting in person and virtually via Zoom on May
2024, August 2024, October 2024 and December 2024. Additionally, the Committee toured the
Village Park construction site twice to observe the project progress.


Regards,



Amy Larsen
2022-2025 Bond Oversight Committee Chair

                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                             Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                                  Minutes for May 7, 2024



The regular monthly meeting of the Bond Oversight Committee was held via video conference and in
person at Fair Oaks Recreation & Park District Office.

For the Record:                    Paula Lucas, Clerk, called the meeting to order at 5:30 pm

Committee Members Present:         Amy Larsen – Chair, Garett McDermid, Kim Sarkovich, Diana
                                   Storo

Committee Members Absent:          Justin Drake – Co-Chair, Dana Woodworker-Negri, Barbara
                                   Sestito and Keith Walter

Staff Present:                     Mike Aho, District Administrator, Paula Lucas, Administrative
                                   Assistant II/Board Clerk

Committee Support:                 None

Guest:                             Ralph Carhart

Members of the Public:             None

Audience/Public Comment:

Amy Larsen, Chair, convened the monthly meeting at 5:31 pm. No public comment. Amy turned
the meeting over to Mike Aho, District Administrator, for construction update and budget status.

Mike shared picture of rendering of construction area in the Village. December 2024/January 2025
(late winter) still on target for completion date. Mike gave a slide presentation of budget and
commented that we have received $1.4 M in interest on our bonds to date. FORPD Board has
various options to handle the remaining project funding and will be discussed at upcoming Board
meetings. Mike stated that it will be $725K for Amphitheatre seating which the Board agrees
needs to be done. Kim asked if naming rights have been discussed and Mike said yes. All options
to bring monies to the project are being reviewed. Arts and Entertainment Manager is gearing up
with programming for the 2025 season. Second annual Garden Gala for the Arts will be held on
May 18, 2024. All other District construction/improvement projects on hold until completion of
the Village project.

Mike then led participants over for a detailed tour of the construction site.



                                                 1
                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                          Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                               Minutes for May 7, 2024



Staff Comments:

None

Committee Comments:

None.

Amy motioned to adjourn the meeting. Kim seconded. Meeting adjourned at 7:02 pm.

Next BOC meeting to be determined at a later date.

                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                            Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                               Minutes for August 6, 2024



The regular monthly meeting of the Bond Oversight Committee was held via video conference and in
person at Fair Oaks Recreation & Park District Office.

For the Record:                    Paula Lucas, Clerk, called the meeting to order at 6:30 pm

Committee Members Present:         Amy Larsen – Chair, Dana Woodworker-Negri, Kim Sarkovich,
                                   Diana Storo

Committee Members Absent:          Justin Drake – Co-Chair, Garett McDermid, Barbara Sestito and
                                   Keith Walter

Staff Present:                     Mike Aho, District Administrator, Paula Lucas, Administrative
                                   Assistant II/Board Clerk

Committee Support:                 None

Members of the Public:             None

Audience/Public Comment:

Amy Larsen, Chair, convened the monthly meeting at 6:31 pm. No public comment. Amy turned
the meeting over to Mike Aho, District Administrator.

Mike presented an update on construction. The Board approved new individual theatre seating.
Work on grading seating area, adding path lights and new seating are in process and will
drastically improve the theatre experience for guests. Mike discussed the road paving projects
and said he met with County staff recently on it. The District decided to end an agreement with
the County on the road paving. This agreement was to pave the road from shoulder to shoulder
but the cost requested from the County will not work with the District. The District will go back to
the originally planned and approved paving. Delays on various projects at the site have been out
of our control. This has been an issue not just with FORPD but everywhere. However, the project
is moving forward and Winter 2024/2025 is the scheduled opening date.

Mike shared that the District is in negotiations to sell a cell tower. Mike also gave an overview on
grant submissions and status to date.

Mike would like to do a site walk through at our next meeting in the Fall.



                                                 1
                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                           Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                              Minutes for August 6, 2024

Staff Comments:

Paula provided the next tentatively confirmed BOC meeting dates for planning purposes. October
1, 2024, December 3, 2024 and February 4, 2025.

Committee Comments:

None.


Amy motioned to adjourn the meeting. Dana seconded. Meeting adjourned at 7:19 pm.

Next BOC meeting to be held October 1, 2024 ~ 6:30 pm in person at the District office and via
video conference.

                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT

                            Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                               Minutes for October 1, 2024



The regular monthly meeting of the Bond Oversight Committee was held via video conference and in
person at Fair Oaks Recreation & Park District Office.

For the Record:                   Paula Lucas, Clerk, called the meeting to order at 6:00 pm

Committee Members Present:        Amy Larsen – Chair, Garett McDermid, Keith Walter; Dana
                                  Woodworker-Negri, Kim Sarkovich, Diana Storo

Committee Members Absent:         Justin Drake – Co-Chair, Barbara Sestito

Staff Present:                    Mike Aho, District Administrator, Paula Lucas, Administrative
                                  Assistant II/Board Clerk

Committee Support:                None

Members of the Public:            None

Audience/Public Comment:          None

Amy Larsen, Chair, convened the monthly meeting at 6:01 pm. No public comment. Amy turned
the meeting over to Mike Aho, District Administrator.

Before Mike walked Members over for a construction site tour he gave an update on the Chair
Dedication Campaign for the amphitheatre. Information on the chair campaign can be found on
the FORPD website. He also shared with the Committee that at the last FORPD Board meeting
the Board acknowledged Barbara Sestito and thanked her for her many years of dedication not
only to the Fair Oaks Theatre but to Fair Oaks in general.

Meeting was moved across the street for a walk-through of the construction site. Mike spoke
about various grants FORPD has in process – Arts in the Park $16,500 initially granted but updated
amount awarded is $22,771.54; Shine Grant $50K – pending.

Staff Comments:

Paula stated that the next meeting will be December 3, 2024.

None.

                             Bond Oversight Committee Meeting
                               Minutes for December 3, 2024



The regular monthly meeting of the Bond Oversight Committee was held via video conference and in
person at Fair Oaks Recreation & Park District Office.

For the Record:                    Paula Lucas, Clerk, called the meeting to order at 6:30 pm

Committee Members Present:         Amy Larsen – Chair, Justin Drake – Co Chair; Garett McDermid,;
                                   Dana Woodworker-Negri, Kim Sarkovich, Diana Storo, Keith
                                   Walter

Committee Members Absent:          Barbara Sestito

Staff Present:                     Mike Aho, District Administrator, Paula Lucas, Administrative
                                   Assistant II/Board Clerk

Committee Support:                 None

Members of the Public:             None

Audience/Public Comment:           None

Amy Larsen, Chair, convened the monthly meeting at 6:31 pm. No public comment. Amy turned
the meeting over to Mike Aho, District Administrator.

Construction Update

Mike presented an update on status of the construction project and shared a slideshow of stage
and surrounding areas. Full power on site now with LED lighting throughout the facility. Outside
lights in the parks working well. Mike said the Board is discussing the best way to honor and share
memorial plaques. Grading is completed for new seating in Amphitheatre and seats should be
installed in March. Playground area graded and PIP (poured in place) foundation going in soon.
Will eventually look to do an RFP for concessions. Food Handlers permit will be required for all
events when the kitchens are in use. Keith commented on possible requirements for the use of
the kitchen and has concerns. Mike said we are at the preliminary stage in planning all of this out.
Kim commented that it is definitely good to have guidelines figured out prior to opening of facility.
Mike said he will share more information on all of this in future meetings once the FORPD Board
has a clearer direction on it.

EV Charging Station Summary: Cost, Time,
and Policy Guidance
Fair Oaks Recreation & Park District – Village Park & Arts Center Area


⚡ EV Charging Overview – Level 2 Stations
- Type of Charging Installed: Level 2 (240V)

- Typical Charging Speed: 6–10 kilowatt-hours (kWh) per hour

**SMUD Electricity Rates for FORPD (as of 2025):
- October 1 – May 31: $0.1248 per kWh (all hours)
- June 1 – September 30:
  • Monday–Friday, Midnight–Noon and all-day weekends/holidays: $0.1505 per
kWh
  • Monday–Friday, Noon–5 PM and 8 PM–Midnight: $0.2077 per kWh


- Average Charging Duration: 2–4 hours per session (partial charge)


🔋🔋 Average Charging Cost to Users (at Proposed Rate of $0.30/kWh)
 Vehicle            Charge Added      Est. Time          Est. Cost
 Nissan Leaf        20 kWh            2–3 hours          $6.00
 Chevy Bolt EV      30 kWh            3–4 hours          $9.00
 Tesla Model 3      35 kWh            3–5 hours          $10.50
 Long Range
 Ford Mustang       40 kWh            4–6 hours          $12.00
 Mach-E
� Most users do not charge from 0% to 100%—they typically “top off” from 20%
to 80%, depending on trip needs.
⏳ Idle Time & Turnover Management
- Vehicles may remain plugged in after charging is complete, tying up spaces

- Recommend implementing Idle Fees to increase availability:

 • Grace Period: 15 minutes after full charge

 • Idle Fee: $2.00 per hour thereafter


💡💡 Board Policy Considerations
- Suggested Usage Fee: $0.30 per kWh

- Suggested Idle Fee: $2.00 per hour, post 15-minute grace period

- Signage should clearly communicate pricing, idle fees, and time limits

- Integrate enforcement tools via charging app or local signage


📌📌 Summary Statement
The average EV user at the Fair Oaks Village Park Charging Stations can expect
to pay $6–$12 per session, with charging typically completed in 2–4 hours.
Implementing idle fees ensures charger turnover, supports equitable access, and
aligns with best practices from other agencies. Revenue generated from the
charging stations will help offset the costs associated with maintaining and
managing the EV infrastructure, including utility expenses, monitoring, and
potential equipment servicing.
              STAFF REPORT

      Meeting Date:      May 21, 2025

      To:                Board of Directors

      From:              Mike J. Aho, District Administrator

      Subject:           Discussion and Possible Approval of the Logo and
                         Branding for the Fair Oaks Performing Arts Center

      Prepared By:       Jen Schuler - Arts & Entertainment Manager

I.      Recommendation
        Staff recommends that the Board of Directors approve the proposed logo and
        branding package for the Fair Oaks Performing Arts Center as presented. This
        approval will allow staff to move forward with consistent branding efforts across
        all marketing platforms and physical assets in preparation for the center’s grand
        opening.

II.     Background
        The Fair Oaks Performing Arts Center is approaching its grand opening, scheduled
        for late summer 2025. As part of the center’s launch, a professional branding
        package has been developed to support marketing, public recognition, and long-
        term identity.

        Staff contracted with GreyDot Media through a Marketing RFP— a local marketing
        and design firm— to create a visual identity that reflects the center’s role as a
        vibrant, inclusive hub for the performing arts. The development process included
        stakeholder input, market research, and iterative design reviews to ensure
        alignment with the center’s mission and the broader goals of the Fair Oaks
        Recreation & Park District.

III.    Problem /Situation/ Request
        With the grand opening and related promotions rapidly approaching, it is
        essential to establish a consistent and compelling brand identity. The proposed
        branding package includes a logo suite, editable color palette, typography, and
        usage guidelines for use across signage, print materials, digital assets,
        merchandise, and internal documents.

        Following a review with the Community Resource Committee, both the
        committee and staff recommend that the Board approve the proposed logo. This
approval will allow staff to move forward with the branding rollout and begin
implementation across all public-facing platforms and materials. It will also
enable the development of the Fair Oaks Performing Arts Center website—an
essential tool for public engagement, event promotion, and ticket sales.

IV.    Financial Analysis
The design and development of the branding package were funded through the
ARPA (American Rescue Plan Act) grant awarded to the District in 2024. A
portion of these funds was previously allocated by the Board specifically for
marketing services related to the Performing Arts Center.

No additional financial allocation is required at this time. Future implementation
costs—such as signage, print production, and merchandise—will be managed
within the existing Arts & Entertainment and Administration budgets.


Respectfully Submitted,



Mike J. Aho
District Administrator
                  STAFF REPORT

       Meeting Date:        May 21, 2025
       To:                  Board of Directors
       From:                Michael J. Aho, District Administrator
       Subject:             Bannister Park Disc Golf Course Feasibility Study
       Prepared By:         Sean Ventura, Parks & Facilities Manager
 I.    Recommendation
       Staff does not currently have a recommendation in association with this feasibility
       study.

II.    Background
       Staff was directed at the March meeting of the Board of Directors to obtain the services
       of a professional consultant to determine the feasibility of Bannister Park as a potential
       location for a disc golf course.

III.   Problem /Situation/ Request
       The feasibility study provided by Whirlwind Disc Golf states that Bannister Park is not an
       ideal location for a disc golf course. Some of the issues identified within the park
       include, elevation changes as a potential safety hazard in some, but not all, locations,
       existing uses of the park, the small footprint of the area, limited parking, encroachment
       upon park land by outside parties, seasonal weather effects, and several other factors.

       At this time there is no request associated with this report, as the Board only instructed
       staff to acquire a professional opinion on the feasibility of the park.

IV.    Financial Analysis
       The overall quote for the feasibility study was $1000, plus $0.75 per mile over the first
       100 miles. The final invoice has not been received, but staff estimate the cost to be
       approximately $1017.25.

Respectfully Submitted,


Michael J. Aho
District Administrator

Attachment A – Bannister Park – Site Assessment
Bannister Park, Fair Oaks
SITE OVERVIEW
Site Characteristics

The potential disc golf course site is in a park located at
the southern end of Bannister Avenue in Fair Oaks, an
unincorporated community in Sacramento County, CA.

The park is roughly 10 acres. The sports field reduces
the potential course area by roughly 2.5 acres. Various other elements, including the parking lot, reduce the
potential area by another .75 acres. On the eastern side of the park, a creek and numerous “no trespassing”
signs may also reduce the potential area by 2+ acres, but this Site Assessment does not exclude that area.




Various elements, outlined in gray above, reduce the assessed area from about 10 acres to about 7 acres.

                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
There are many signs in the eastern quarter of the park stating that the area is private, not public, space. Even if
the signs are invalid, it does suggest that whoever posted the signs is unlikely to welcome more usage.

Although it’s beyond the scope and expertise of this assessment, the undeveloped park areas don’t appear to
have significant habitat or species concerns that would require any direct mitigation. The park district is
encouraged to conduct its own analysis, as needed, regarding potential environmental impact and mitigations.

The terrain ranges from level, well-maintained sports fields to steep, overgrown, unimproved drainage.

Most of the potential disc golf areas fall into one of three terrain types, generally described here:
•   Terrain type 1 (pink): Relatively flat areas with maintained trees, near popular existing uses. Disc golf
    installation would be easy, but constrained by integration with existing uses. Player appeal would be
    limited.
•   Terrain type 2 (violet): Moderately sloped areas with some dense growth and some social trails. Disc
    golf installation would be significantly complex, laborious, and costly. Player appeal would be good.
•   Terrain type 3 (purple): Severely sloped areas with difficult access or high erosion risk, and some
    extremely dense growth. Disc golf installation would be complex and expensive, with greater
    maintenance costs. Player appeal would be good.




Three distinct types of terrain with varying degrees of adaptability regarding existing park uses and the
cost and complexity of installing and maintaining disc golf.


                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Examples of “terrain type 1”




Examples of “terrain type 2”




Examples of “terrain type 3”




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Disc Golf in the Community

Disc golf is popular in the Sacramento region, likely the most popular region in California in terms of players and
courses. There are roughly 30 courses with 30 miles of Fair Oaks.

Fair Oaks itself has one disc golf course, located at Miller Park. The course has recently been scaled back in
response to safety and displacement concerns expressed by other park users. Its future seems tenuous.

Near Fair Oaks, there are several courses within 10 miles. The closest is a 9-hole course at Carmichael Park in
Carmichael, a popular community park with many other activities. The most prominent is the 18-hole course
known as Shady Oaks, one of the busiest courses in California, located in Orangevale Community Park in Citrus
Heights. Shady Oaks is located in a mixed-use park, but the limited number of adjacent park features and the
large amount of usage makes it feel similar to a disc-golf-only space.

Various skill levels are served by local courses, so there’s not an especially strong need/opportunity to satisfy a
particular demand. Generally, however, there’s a lack of entry-level courses in most regions, including
Sacramento – although Sacramento is better than most in this regard.




There are several courses within 10 miles of Bannister Park, including Miller, Carmichael, and Shady Oaks.

*see UDisc Growth Report 2025




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
SUITABILITY & CAPACITY
The central feature of this Site Assessment is a Google map which rates various sections within the site for
Suitability and Capacity.

The goal of this assessment is to describe how well disc golf might work in each section of a given site. It’s only
an evaluation. It is not a proposal to use all sections evaluated at the site, nor should it be interpreted as a
recommendation to install the maximum number of potential holes.

The sections, and their respective suitability, generally align with the terrain types described above – e.g., the
better terrain generally correlates to better Suitability. However, the section-to-section Suitability ratings
account for many other factors, not just terrain. Also, the section-by-section approach provides a way to group
adjacent sections to form the most reasonable course area.

About Suitability
“Suitability” is rated on a 9-step color spectrum from “Fantastic” (dark green) to “So-So” (yellow) to “No Way!” (dark red).

Suitability incorporates many factors. From a community perspective, these factors include safety, environment, and
integration. (“Integration” meaning how well disc golf may fit with existing experiences.) From a player perspective, these
factors include skill levels for desired and/or likely participants, flight variety, visual appeal, access, weather, safety, and
security. From a client perspective, these factors include installation cost and effort, maintenance cost and effort, and
liability.

The Suitability rating is an initial indicator, not a static predictor. A professional course designer can ensure, for example,
that even sections rated “Poor” can meet high standards of safety and integration, given proper investments, mitigations,
and any necessary compromises to player appeal. Also, custom features such as tree planting, erosion mgmt, and enhanced
tees or greens can greatly mitigate issues and raise the quality of the experience.

Nonetheless, the overall appeal and success of the course tends to correlate to these ratings.

About Capacity
The map also indicates the potential Capacity – i.e., the potential number of holes – if a course were to have holes in that
section. The Capacity rating is based on a rough sense of potential, not an actual design. During the design process, many
things will be taken into consideration to determine how many holes would actually work well in any given area, leading to a
final layout that will serve all stakeholders well.

The footprint of a disc golf course will vary depending on the terrain, skill levels, and other factors. A common rule of thumb
is one acre per hole. However, elite competitive courses such as Gleneagles in San Francisco use up to 3 acres per hole.
On the other hand, beginner-friendly courses such as Santa Anita in Sacramento use as little as 1/3 of an acre per hole.
Some parks have nothing more than 2-3 practice baskets, the disc golf equivalent of a traditional golf putting green.

The vast majority of courses are found in public spaces where disc golf shares space with other “pass-through” activities
such as walking and biking. To function harmoniously, these shared spaces cannot be monopolized by any activity.

Note that sections with a Suitability rating of “no way!” are not numbered, and do not estimate hole capacity. Golf is not
feasible in these areas at all.




                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Suitability Assessment

This assessment evaluates 12 sections within the potential site, per screenshot below. The Suitability ratings
are intended to help stakeholders envision the most likely areas to be used.

Most sections at Bannister Park rate “sub-par” or “poor”.




Screenshot of Google map w/ color-coded Suitability ratings. Basically: green best, yellow good, orange poor, red bad.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Capacity Assessment

These Capacity estimates assume the holes will measure from 100-200 feet, serving beginner to lower-
intermediate skill levels. The potential number of holes is shown on the map, and also in the bullet list below.




Screenshot of Google map which shows how many holes might fit in the section.

…

Pro tip: When viewing the Google map, toggle the various layers on/off to better understand the assessment.




                        Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Suitability & Capacity Assessment (bullet list)

Each of the sections also indicates the potential number of holes in that area. The sections are ordered roughly
clockwise, starting at the parking lot near the pond. All sections indicate the potential number of holes the
space can support, except “no holes” are necessary in the sections rated “dubious” or “no way!”:
    •   Section 1: 1-2 holes / .6 acres (“sub-par!”)             •   Section 7: 1-2 holes / .2 acres (“poor”)
    •   Section 2: 1-3 holes / .5 acres (“so-so”)                •   Section 8: 1-3 holes / .5 acres (“so-so”)
    •   Section 3: 2-4 holes / 1.1 acres (“sub-par”)             •   Section 9: 1-2 holes / .3 acres (“poor”)
    •   Section 4: 1-2 holes / .3 acres (“sub-par”)              •   Section 10: 0 holes / .1 acres (“dubious”)
    •   Section 5: 1-3 holes / .8 acres (“sub-par”)              •   Section 11: 0-1 holes / .2 acres (“poor”)
    •   Section 6: 1-2 holes / .6 acres (“dubious”)              •   Section 12: 2-3 holes / .5 acres (“poor”)

The sections highlighted above may also be good locations for practice baskets – putting back and forth like a
putting green at a traditional golf course – rather than having standard tees for longer throws.

As noted in the “About Capacity” info above, this does not mean we should use the maximum number of holes
in every potential space – 25 holes at this site would not make sense! – but rather to provide a way, by
combining adjacent sections, to achieve a suitable number of holes for the intended scale, skills, and budget.

Generally, a disc golf course should have at least 6 holes in order to feel like you’ve played a round of golf, to
get a score that you can compare to previous rounds of golf on the course. Anything less than 6 holes usually
feels like throwing practice – which isn’t a bad thing – but not like playing a round of golf.


…

Bottom line: Overall, the site is workable, but far from ideal for disc golf. It’s “so-so” at best.

The best sections are generally contiguous, so it should be possible to string together a few appealing holes
without using much, if any, of the less suitable terrain.

How many of the 12 sections will or should be used? That depends on many things that are not decided yet:
stakeholder input which may further inform Suitability or Capacity, the type of course (short<>long,
simple<>complex, local<>destination), the budget, and other factors. Also, a future design may, for various
reasons – e.g., flow, maintenance, access – choose to trade some higher-rated sections for lower-rated (yet
viable) sections.

Below, the Preliminary Recommendations provide more detail on which areas might be used.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
PRELIMINARY RECOMMENDATIONS
The following recommendations are preliminary and may change significantly during further analysis, design,
and discussion. They’re intended to help identify next steps in the planning process.

Basic Questions / Key Variables

How much suitable space is available? How much highly suitable space?
    •   Space is very limited.
           o Maximum 5 acres of potential acres of space better than “dubious” (the 2nd worse rating).
           o Maximum 4 acres of potential acres better than “poor” (the 3rd worse rating)
    •   There is no space that rates better than “so-so”.

How much money is available for course design, equipment, and installation?
    •   Specific budget unknown at this time.
    •   To explore common course costs, check out the Whirlwind Cost Worksheet.

What skill level(s) could be served?
    •   Given the size of the space, short holes make the most sense (100-200 feet), serving beginners to
        lower intermediate players.
    •   Given the slope and dense growth in the most suitable parts of the site, beginners may not find it
        appealing unless lots of clearing, retaining, and trails are provided.
            o If available budget doesn’t support these investments, then the likely participants will be
                existing players in the recreational to lower-intermediate range. Worth noting that this may
                seem like a narrow slice of the potential player community, but these skill levels are the most
                common skill levels in the sport – roughly 40-50% of all players.

Are there any other major variables that might impact the location, scale, complexity, or style of the course –
e.g., parking, bathrooms, security, trails, habitat, future development plans, existing uses, future uses,
temporary uses, etc.
    •   Parking already appears to be a challenge on days with nice weather, especially weekends. If a good
        course is provided, it’s likely that parking pressure will spill into adjacent neighborhood streets for 5-10
        hours each week, on average – even greater during nice weather.
            o Note: To estimate peak parking impact, divide daily rounds by 4 – given that players don’t all
                play at the same time, and sometimes carpool. For example, if we estimate 60 rounds on a
                summer weekend day – a reasonable guess for a decent course in this region – parking impact
                would peak at around 15 vehicles. (This pressure, if manageable at all, may preclude organized
                disc golf events on weekends because parking could not handle all the players being present at
                the same time.)
    •   Without substantial investment, the steep areas around the creek & gullies will be difficult and
        possibly unsafe to traverse, especially during the wet season.
           o It’s possible to design the course where the vast majority of throws stay out of those areas,
                but errant shots and unlucky rolls/deflections are not entirely avoidable. Lacking such
                investment, the feasible course area would have to be reduced substantially.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
            o   If rainfall is particularly heavy, the course may need to be closed for a few days/weeks each
                year.
•   Existing social trails are not numerous, but they do pass through some of the more suitable disc golf
    areas – especially the one leading from the sports field down to the bike trail, going between sections
    #5 and #8.
             o It’s possible to safely design without impacting these trails.
             o It’s also possible to relocate such trails to enable less impact from disc golf. Generally,
                 however, budget and public relations are good reasons to avoid impacting existing uses
                 whenever possible.
•   The “no trespassing” zone on the eastern quarter of the park – over 2 acres – is a good news, bad news
    situation.
            o Good news is that it’s more separated from the busier parts of the park, less likely to alter
                existing experiences.
            o Bad new is that the people who put the signs there probably would oppose a course.
• As noted above, it’s beyond the scope and expertise of this assessment to determine authoritatively
    whether a disc golf course would cause any environmental harm to important species or habitat.
    However, given the lack of frequent human presence in the eastern half of the park, it may be worth
    having an expert take a closer look.




COURSE OPTIONS

Given the information above – Suitability, Capacity, basic considerations and most impactful variables – what
might disc golf look like at Bannister Park?

Here are three options, from most to least ambitious.

Standard disclaimer: These options should be considered preliminary, to provide perspective and help inform
productive conversations among stakeholders. A professional designer should be enlisted to apply all variables
in a balanced and thoughtful manner, working collaboratively and iteratively to produce the best possible disc
golf experience for players, other park users, and staff.

Budget estimates assume professional course design, standard course equipment (tee pads, baskets, signs),
volunteer labor for clearing and simple tasks, licensed contractor(s) for complex tasks. To explore common
course costs, check out the Whirlwind Cost Worksheet.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Option A: 9-hole technical course on the east side
    -    Unique characteristics
            o Largest footprint, including “no trespassing zone” and all creek/gully areas
            o “Technical” golf is the most appealing to players (“technical” means lots of obstacles, requiring
                 curving throws.)
            o Greatest parking pressure
            o Substantial installation and maintenance cost, given the rough, steep terrain and necessary
                 mitigations re creeks/gullies
    -    Details
            o Sections: #2, #3, #4, #5, #7, #8, #9
            o Number of holes: 9-12
            o Distance: 100-200 feet
            o Tees: 1 per hole
            o Footprint: ~4 acres
            o Skill levels served best: recreational to intermediate
            o Usage (assuming parking capacity): 25-75 players per day depending on season and day of
                 week, assuming course conditions always good.
            o Budget: $40K-100K+




Option A: Very appealing 9-hole course in eastern half of the park. Very high level of installation complexity, cost.



                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Option B: 6-hole technical course in the northeast corner
-   Unique characteristics
            o Small footprint, including roughly half of “no trespassing zone”
            o “Technical” golf is the most appealing to players (“technical” means lots of obstacles, requiring
               curving throws.)
            o Uses creek area, but less extensive and severe than Option A
            o Avoids existing social trails pretty well
            o Significant installation and maintenance cost, given the rough, steep terrain and necessary
               mitigations re creeks/gullies
-   Details
            o Sections: #2, #3, and half of #5
            o Number of holes: 5-7
            o Distance: 100-200 feet
            o Tees: 1 per hole
            o Footprint: ~2 acres
            o Skill levels served best: recreational to intermediate
            o Usage (assuming parking capacity): 20-50 players per day depending on season and day of
               week, assuming course conditions always good.
            o Budget: $30K-60K+




Option B: Appealing 6-hole course in eastern half of the park. High level of installation complexity, cost.


                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Option C: Practice baskets in one of three sections
    -    Unique characteristics
            o Putting practice only, not a course
            o Very limited expense, easy to install and maintain
            o Modest appeal to players
            o Little/no significant impact to existing uses
            o No environmental concerns
    -    Details
            o Sections: #1, #4, or #11 – just one section, not all three
            o Number of baskets: 2-4
            o Distance: putting circuit 15-75 feet
            o Tees: 3-6 pavers per basket
            o Footprint: ~.25 acres or less
            o Skill levels served best: recreational to intermediate
            o Usage (assuming parking capacity): 10-25 players per day depending on season and day of
                 week, assuming course conditions always good.
            o Budget: $3K-10K+ (professional design useful, but not essential)




Option C: Putting practice baskets in one of these 3 sections.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
-   A few more notes on practice putting areas
        o Natural or man-made obstacles make it more fun. In this regard, Section #1 is best.
        o Sections #1 and #4 are adjacent to the athletic field. Which might be intuitive – putting
           recreational activities next to each other – but a small percentage of players will disregard the
           rules and throw to these baskets from across the field. (Every barrel has some rotten apples,
           right?)
        o Section #11 has good proximity to parking and less foot traffic than other sections.




                   Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
PHOTOS (w/ comments)




The sports field is the biggest feature in the park.




The edges of the sports field have some nice trees for picnicking – and potentially putting baskets (sections #4, #1).




                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
The transition from sports field to unimproved terrain consists of a relatively narrow grassy areas, which often become
dense and/or steep rather abruptly. Social trails are most prominent in these transitional areas.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
The most ideal space for disc golf is where tree density is moderate and the downslope toward drainage is also moderate.
Note that the first image looks great to a disc golfer – and (next image) it also looks great to people on the trail!




                        Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Even where the underlying terrain is reasonably accommodating – not too steep, with moderate density of mature growth
– a lot of clearing would be needed.




                        Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
East of the sports field, hillsides are often dense and sometimes damp, leading to drainage running north-south through
the entire space. Erosion risk – both environmental and user safety – are significant.


                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
More examples of the overgrowth that would largely need to be cleared to make the area accessible to foot traffic. If one
focuses their vision only on the trees, the spacing is actually quite nice for disc golf.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
So many trespassing warning signs! That are probably on park property!




                        Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
In the northeast corner of the park, beyond several warning signs, a trail leads to what may be someone’s backyard.
But, unlike the southeast corner of the park (below), one does not encounter a fence that clearly indicates a property line.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
As the bike trail makes its way down toward the river, there’s a utility building near the southeast corner of the park.




                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Section 12 is along the Waldorf school’s property line. It important to avoid discs flying over their fence, of course. Also,
the school and its kids might enjoy having a course nearby, or at least some baskets for putting.




                          Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
The quantity and specificity of parking-related signs suggests that parking demand already exceeds capacity.


                         Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
                              These photos are nothing in particular. It’s a nice park!


Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
APPENDIX A: MORE ABOUT DISC GOLF
How Do You Play Disc Golf?

Disc golf is played like traditional golf, except you throw a disc rather than hit a ball. On each hole, the first
shot is thrown from the tee; subsequent shots are thrown from wherever the disc has landed, until
completing the hole by throwing the disc into the basket. The goal is to complete the course in as few shots as
possible.

More info:
   - Introduction from the governing organization of disc golf (comparable to the USGA):
       https://www.pdga.com/introduction
   - Basic rules and courtesy: https://discgolf.com/disc-golf-education-development/disc-golf-rules-for-
       recreational-play/
   - Community club’s introduction to disc golf: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oYcujwAKwVw
   - How Do Golf Discs Fly (long video): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-GwdghSH4rk

Disc Golf Players and Culture

Almost anyone can play disc golf. Age doesn’t matter. Physical and economic barriers are low.

There are roughly 10 million regular disc golfers in the U.S.
   - Frequency: 10% of all players are “Avid” (at least once a week), 60% of all players are “Dedicated” (at
        least once a month), 30% of all players are “Occasional” (at least once a year).
   - Skills: 2% Expert, 13% Advanced, 35% Intermediate, 35% Recreational, 10% Beginner.
   - Yes, there’s a fairly strong correlation between frequency and skill – e.g., most “Avid” players are
        either Expert or Advanced.
   - Over 50% of rounds are played by Intermediate or better players.

Generally, if you see someone playing disc golf, they’re most likely an intermediate or recreational player who
plays at least once a month. Once players get started, they tend to get hooked; they play a lot.

Disc golfers are widely represented across age, race, gender, socioeconomics, and geography. The most highly
represented demographic, relative to actual population is high-school-educated, working-class white males in
their 30s.

As a grassroots sport, disc golf culture largely reflects the culture of the local community. However, there are
some themes and values which do distinguish the sport overall, especially among more experienced players.
    -   Etiquette. Like traditional golf, disc golfers are expected to demonstrate self-officiated integrity, even
        in the most serious competitions.
    -   Courtesy. Most disc golf courses are located in multi-use areas of public parks. The vast majority of disc
        golfers are habitually considerate of other park users.
    -   Inclusiveness. Yes, disc golf’s oldest roots were nurtured by the hippy counter-culture, a contrast to
        the exclusive and expensive reputation of traditional golf. Moreover, as a relatively small sport, disc
        golf’s participants are eager to share their passion with new people.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
    -   Environmental stewardship. Some people refer to disc golf as “throwing a Frisbee while hiking.” Disc
        golfers celebrate the natural space and often volunteer to care for it. Also, disc golf courses are often
        located in previously neglected or troubled spaces, so players take pride in the spaces that the
        community has reclaimed.

More info:
- UDisc Disc Golf Growth Report – lots of data
    -   State of Disc Golf Survey: articles on player demographics:
        https://infinitediscs.com/blog/?s=demographics
    -   A sport that everyone can play: https://infinitediscs.com/blog/courses/disc-golf-a-sport-that-everyone-
        can-play/
    -   Older people playing disc golf: http://lompocrecord.com/and-thriving-donald-shinn-still-enjoys-disc-
        golf/article_fc2399e1-bb22-5424-8948-413b4975699c.html
    -   Young people playing disc golf
        o   https://www.ourvalleyvoice.com/2017/10/12/junior-disc-golfers-compete-california-
            championships-mooney-grove/
        o   https://www.facebook.com/communityfirsthealthplans/videos/1354827844540517/
        o   http://bakersfieldnow.com/news/local/bakersfield-teen-sets-new-disc-throw-world-record
        o   https://uplaydiscgolf.org/
        o   Kids and disc golf culture: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JSTsXJGuVIc&feature=youtu.be


Issues & Perceptions

When a disc golf course is installed, some people are excited about the chance to test their disc-throwing skills
in a natural space, while others – especially those unfamiliar with disc golf -- may have reservations. Concerns
are usually in four areas:
    •   Safety. No sport or recreation is absolutely risk-free. Each year, roughly 4 trillion – yes, trillion – disc
        golf throws are made on courses in multi-use areas (over 10,000 courses x 365 days x 25 rounds x 50
        throws). Based on available data, the average number of severe injuries to players or passers-by is less
        than one per year. Nonetheless, there are ways to reduce this historically tiny risk even further,
        including the installation of informational signage and hiring a professional designer to ensure shots
        don’t fly into trouble spots.
    •   Environment. Disc golf’s impact on the environment is far lighter than most sports and recreations,
        many of which require elimination of natural space. On well-designed courses, the impact is limited to
        foot traffic and maybe marks on trees from deflecting discs. Habitat impacts are extremely rare. When
        needed, a professional designer can provide straightforward mitigations to potential erosion and
        impacts to flora and fauna.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
   •   Misbehavior. Often, disc golf courses are a solution to misbehavior such as crime, dumping, and
       encampments. Sometimes however, despite the overall disc golf culture of stewardship and courtesy,
       some disc golf newbies may behave badly, just like bad apples in any group. Rest assured that
       experienced players find such misbehavior completely unacceptable – and will be the first to provide
       guidance toward proper behavior.
   •   Congestion / Monopolization. Disc golf is a pass-through activity which integrates well with other
       pass-through activities. (A “pass-through activity” is where people continue through the space rather
       than remaining stationary for more than a minute or two – e.g., walkers, bikers.) Because most courses
       are installed in multi-use space, most disc golfers have become accustomed to waiting to throw when
       someone is passing through or near a hole. At a typical course, disc golf’s usage density averages less
       than one person per acre, and only 2-3 people per acre during peak times. A professional designer is
       key to minimizing any integration issues where disc golf is added to a multi-use park.

More info:
   -   Public perception is increasingly positive: https://parkeddiscgolf.org/2019/10/14/disc-golf-is-winning-
       hearts-and-minds-new-study-
       shows/?fbclid=IwAR3vJPCFgFSmSOvPaeXigh3SdWgLM9n0f292_huUpDvxgvTnb1TyfucGey8
   -   Mental health benefits, ala Chef Andrew Zimmern: https://discgolf.ultiworld.com/2016/07/28/bizarre-
       foods-andrew-zimmern-disc-golf-nerd-just-like/
   -   New course in Santa Rosa helps with homeless concern:
       https://www.pressdemocrat.com/lifestyle/9956867-181/why-sonoma-county-disc-golfers
   -   Disc golf actually benefits the environment: https://www.capenews.net/sandwich/news/sandwich-
       disc-golf-course-proposal-rises-again/article_0af12805-16ca-5e01-bf9f-788138476eab.html
   -   Costa Rica country club which boasts disc golf as eco-friendly alternative to traditional golf:
       https://www.facebook.com/RobGreenfield/videos/1169764313150645/?hc_ref=NEWSFEED
   -   Experienced course designers take safety seriously: https://www.discgolfpark.com/2018/03/14/disc-
       golf-course-safety-defines-legacy/



Disc Golf Organizations

Professional Disc Golf Association: https://www.pdga.com/
   -   The PDGA is the non-profit governing body of the sport, similar to the USGA’s role for traditional golf.
       It sets the rules and technical standards, sanctions major competitions, and provides worldwide
       promotion.


Disc Golf Pro Tour: https://www.dgpt.com/

    - Reichmuth Disc Golf Club
    - Folsom Disc Golf Club

Products, Manufacturers
   -   Disc golf has annual retail sales over $500M. There are many major golf disc manufacturers (and a lot
       of smaller ones), plus companies that make custom bags, carts, accessories, attire, etc.


                      Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
   -   Biggest manufacturer: https://www.innovadiscs.com/
   -   Major basket manufacturer / sport founder: https://discgolf.com/
   -   Successful online retail store: https://otbdiscs.com/


More general information about disc golf:
   -   Disc golf from a parks department perspective:
       https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=XwcG2L3_aoo
   -   Versatility of Disc Golf: https://infinitediscs.com/blog/courses/versatility-of-disc-golf/
   -   Community benefits: https://infinitediscs.com/blog/courses/disc-golf-benefits-for-the-community/
   -   Educational programs: https://edgediscgolf.org/
   -   US Championship – spectators, viewers, and charitable tradition: https://usdgc.com/posts/usdgc-
       viewers-charitable-donations-recap/?fbclid=IwAR3h27bOYobalvFssj8JGy8qZE18Mgn73mhZqRS4ZkGg-
       WrJ-Hwh8Q_LxDA
   -   Playing disc golf at Stafford Lake, Marin County Park:
       http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t16CmbLwGrw
   -   Disc golf looking for a home in the Bay Area, back in 1978:
       https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=418d879mufQ
   -   Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disc_golf




                      Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
APPENDIX B: COURSE EQUIPMENT AND COSTS

Tee Pads

The tee is where the hole begins.

Tee pads are most commonly 5’ wide by 10’ long composed
of textured concrete. They may be smaller on small courses
and bigger on big courses. Concrete is durable, low-
maintenance, and consistent in varied weather.

Other tee surfaces include pavers, artificial turf, and rubber
mats. Turf is popular on commercial courses for aesthetics
and performance, but requires more maintenance. Rubber
mats perform fairly well, somewhat easier to install,
recyclable; but can be slippery when wet or dusty. Pavers
can be great if professionally installed, but tend to have
maintenance/safety issues more quickly than other options.
Some courses use unimproved ground rather than a pad,
but it will likely perform and look bad if played frequently.

Depending on the terrain and weather, installation may
require drainage or retaining or other customizations to
ensure long-term viability.
                                                                 Concrete tee pad with textured surface for year-round
Multiple tee pads per hole increase variety and help             performance, unfinished. One of several tee pad
                                                                 material options.
mitigate potential erosion.



Tee Signs

A tee sign should be placed at each tee to identify the hole
number, the par, the distance, and sometimes to illustrate
the recommended flight path(s).

Tee signs are typically coated metal with color graphics,
roughly 12”x18” vertical, mounted about 4’ high on an
anchored wood post, located safely behind or to the side of
the tee pad. Options include UV coatings and polycarbonate
to help protect signs from sun and vandalism. Some courses
use alternatives, including mounting signs on tree stumps,
painting numbers on rocks, or etching the number into the
concrete pad.




                                                                 Example of a tee sign illustrating the hole.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Baskets

To complete the hole, the disc must land in the
basket. The hanging chains act as a backstop to
help the disc stay within the basket.

Baskets are sturdy, built with galvanized steel.
They stand about 60” tall and 28” wide, and weigh
50-60 lbs.
The basket pipe slides into a sleeve anchored in a
concrete base 18-24” deep, secured by a
padlocked collar. The basket can be easily moved
by one person from one sleeve to another, by
unlocking, lifting out of current position, sliding
into a new position, and relocking. Custom
branding and powder-coated colors are available
from most basket manufacturers.

Baskets, per typical layouts, are usually installed  Example of a disc golf target
in multiples of 9, one per hole. (For some reason,
many top courses have more than 18 holes but not in multiples of 9.) Multiple sleeves on each hole increase
variety and mitigate potential erosion. Many courses also have additional basket(s) for putting practice.


Other Costs

Professional disc golf course designers ensure that your course is as enjoyable, safe, and sustainable as
possible. Designer fees vary widely, depending on the reputation of the designer, the complexity of the terrain
and installation, and the market in that region. Leonard Muise of Whirlwind Disc Golf is considered one of the
top designers in the sport, perhaps top in the western U.S. Whirlwind’s standard fee is $3000 + $250-750 per
hole, depending on the complexity of the terrain and the number of tees and sleeves per hole.

For basic installations, common construction materials will be needed – lumber, concrete, gravel, etc. Other
common costs include a welcome sign at the first hole, padlocks for the basket sleeves, and extra basket(s) for
practicing putting off the course.

Less common costs include benches, bag holders, directional signs between holes, tree planting and/or tree
screening, gravel or pavers around tee pads, path demarcation, custom green complexes, and various terrain-
driven features like retaining walls, steps, and bridges.

Labor costs are a huge variable. In many communities, the local disc golfers provide skilled, volunteer labor.


Total Cost – Various Levels (ballpark estimates do not include labor)

Bare Bones – $6,000 for every 9 holes
- Good quality baskets, no practice basket
- No tee pads, signs, or extra sleeves
- Amateur designer



                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
    Most Typical – $12,000 for every 9 holes
    - Good quality baskets, plus one practice basket
    - One tee pad and one simple tee sign per hole, two sleeves per hole
    - One unique installation feature – e.g., raised basket
    - Amateur designer

Excellent Local Course – $18,000 for every 9 holes
    - Best quality baskets, plus two practice baskets
    - Two tee pads per hole, durable custom tee signs, three sleeves per hole
    - Welcome sign, caution signs
    - Multiple unique installation features – e.g., raised basket(s), retaining around hillside tee box(es)
    - Professional designer to ensure the course is fun, safe, and sustainable

Regional Standout – $30,000+ for every 9 holes
   - Best quality baskets, plus two practice baskets
   - Two tee pads per hole, durable custom tee signs, three or four sleeves per hole
   - Welcome sign, directional signs, caution signs
   - Many unique installation features – several raised baskets, several tee box retaining, some complex
       landscaping, benches, bag-holders, new trees, etc.
   - Serves many skills well, ranging from novice play to regional competition
   - Professional designer to ensure the course is fun, safe, and sustainable

World-Class – Total Cost $250K+
   - The course equipment is a small part of the total cost
   - Manicured tee and green areas
   - Up to 4 tee pads per hole and 5 sleeves per hole
   - May include clearing, earth-moving, extensive complex landscaping, new planting, bridges, stairs, etc
   - Accommodates media and gallery for elite competition
   - Top professional designer


Whirlwind’s ala carte pricing tool: https://tinyurl.com/y6cqevko

Whirlwind’s online tool has current estimates for all common costs. You can easily change features – e.g.,
different baskets, different tee pads – and immediately see the budget impact.




                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Exceptional custom tee box at Gleneagles, San Francisco.   Exceptional custom “green” on a course in the Midwest.




                        Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
APPENDIX C: DESIGN PRINCIPLES, PROCESS, TIMELINE




             Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf
Design Process
When designing or redesigning a disc golf course, Whirlwind generally follows these steps:
  1. Stakeholder Meeting: Goals and Constraints
  2. Community Context
  3. Site Exploration – Suitability and Capacity
  4. Artifact: Site Assessment Report
  5. Stakeholder Meeting: Feasibility Review
  6. Site Exploration – Layout Superlatives and Constraints
  7. Layout – Basic Routing
  8. Layout – Hole concepts
  9. Layout – User Testing
      (steps 8 & 9 may be repeated)
  10. Layout – Refinements
  11. Artifact: Course Design (Draft)
  12. Stakeholder Meeting: Design Feedback
  13. Layout – Modifications
  14. Layout – User Testing
  15. Layout – Refinements
  16. Artifact: Course Design (Final)
  17. Stakeholder Meeting: Design Approval

Typical Course Design Timeline (assuming “Regional Leader” or higher standard)
    -   Site Assessment: 1-2 weeks
    -   Draft Layout: 4-6 weeks
    -   Final Layout: 2-3 weeks


Typical Course Installation Timeline (assuming “Regional Leader” or higher standard, some tasks may
be concurrent)
    - Equipment Procurement: 1-4 weeks (supply chain lead times TBD)
    - Hole Illustrations / Graphic Design: 3-4 weeks
    - Sign fabrication: 2-3 weeks
    - Fairway preparation (widest range of potential time): 1-12 weeks
    - Basket installation: 1-2 weeks
    - Tee installation: 1-6 weeks
    - Sign installation: 1-2 weeks
    - “Unique Installation Features” (e.g., raised baskets, retaining, stairs, bridges, trails, other custom
       landscaping, new trees, etc): TBD

More info re course development:
- https://www.pdga.com/course-development
- https://www.innovadiscs.com/course-development/
- https://infinitediscs.com/blog/courses/design-tips/
- http://www.discgolfcoursedesigners.org/wiki/index.php/Main_Page



                       Bannister Park Site Assessment – May 2025 – Whirlwind Disc Golf

 I.     Recommendation
        Elect the Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation (FORF) Board of Directors for FY
        2025-2026 (Attachment A).

II.     Background
        The Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Bylaws require an annual meeting of the Fair
        Oaks Recreation Foundation be held by the 4th Monday of June for the purpose of
        electing the Foundation Board of Directors and the officers of the Foundation, and
        for conducting any other business or transactions deemed necessary.

        The Fair Oaks Recreation and Park District Board of Directors also serves as the
        FORF Board of Directors. The FORF typically follows the same configuration as the
        FORPD Board officers for Chair and Vice-Chair, which for 2025 is as follows:

        Delinda Tamagni, Chair
        John O’Farrell, Vice-Chair

        The other officers rotate annually, and if the FORF Board follows the same process,
        the other officers would be as follows:

        Raymond James Irwin/Darren Mounts – Chief Financial Officers
        Ralph Carhart – Board Secretary
III.   Problem /Situation/ Request
       The Recreation Foundation Board must elect its Board and Officers for FY 2025-
       2026 (Attachment A).


IV.    Financial Analysis
       There is no financial impact to the District resulting from the recommended action
       within the Recreation Foundation. The election of Foundation Officers is an annual
       action.


Respectfully Submitted,

                                        Page 2 of 2
            STAFF REPORT

 Meeting Date:       May 21, 2025

 To:                 Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Board of Directors

 From:               Michael Aho – District Administrator

 Subject:            Transfer of Funds from the Fair Oaks Youth Advisory
                     Board Account to the Fair Oaks Recreation and Park
                     District General Fund

 Prepared by:        Mary Stoakley, Recreation Supervisor

I.     Recommendations
       Approve Resolution No. 052125-03, transferring funds from Fair Oaks
       Youth Advisory Board (FOYAB) Foundation Account to the Fair Oaks
       Recreation and Park District (FORPD) Miscellaneous Revenue Account
       (97979000). (Attachment A)

II.    Background
       In 2010 the Board of Directors approved a resolution to have FOYAB
       operate from FORPD general funds, with the intent of FOYAB paying back
       the District at the end of the year. This decision was made to allow the
       Recreation staff access to purchasing supplies via Cal Cards and to allow for
       ease in budget tracking. Currently, FOYAB submits and gains internal
       approval of their program budget along with the other recreation programs,
       events, and classes implemented by the FORPD Recreation staff.

IV.    Problem/ Situation/ Request
       In FY 23/24, FOYAB deposited revenues into the Fair Oaks Youth Advisory
       Board (FOYAB) Foundation Account and FORPD Miscellaneous Revenue
       Account (97979000), initially all expenses were advanced from the FORPD
       General Fund.

IV.    Financial Analysis
       Total FOYAB revenues for FY 24/25 deposited into both the Fair Oaks Youth
       Advisory Board (FOYAB) Foundation Account and FORPD Miscellaneous
       Revenue Account (97979000) were $1,856.22. Total FOYAB expenditures
       for FY 24/25 advanced by the FORPD General Fund were $2,154.93, less
       $1,856.22 deposited into Fair Oaks Youth Advisory Board (FOYAB)
       Foundation Account Miscellaneous Revenue Account (97979000).
       Therefore, $2,154.93 must be reimbursed to the Miscellaneous Revenue
       Account (97979000).

Respectfully Submitted,



Michael Aho
District Administrator

Attachment A:        Resolution 052125-03, to Recognize Revenue from the
                     2024-2025 Fair Oaks Youth Advisory Board Programs and to
                     Reflect the Transfer of Funds from the Foundation Account to
                     the Miscellaneous Revenue Account to Offset Expenses
                     Initially Incurred by the District
                           RESOLUTION NO. 052125-03

                 BEFORE THE GOVERNING BOARD OF THE
                  FAIR OAKS RECREATION FOUNDATION

                     County of Sacramento, State of California

   TO RECOGNIZE REVENUE FROM THE 2024-2025 FAIR OAKS YOUTH
 ADVISORY BOARD PROGRAMS AND TO REFLECT THE TRANSFER OF
   FUNDS FROM THE FAIR OAKS YOUTH ADVISORY BOARD (FOYAB)
 FOUNDATION TO THE FAIR OAKS RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT
MISCELLANEOUS REVENUE ACCOUNT TO OFFSET EXPENSES INITIALLY
                  INCURRED BY THE DISTRICT

WHEREAS, The Board of Directors, for the Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation, passed
Resolution No. 052125-03, on May 21, 2025, recognizing the revenues from the
2024-2025 Fair Oaks Youth Advisory Board programs and reflect the transfer of funds
from the Foundation Account to the Miscellaneous Revenue Account to offset expenses
initially incurred by the District.

WHEREAS, the procedure to allow the transfer of these now recognized and released
funds requires action by the Board of Directors of the Foundation.

THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED that the Fair Oaks Recreation
Foundation Board of Directors approves the transfer as follows:

A. The sum of $2,154.93 be deducted from the Fair Oaks Youth Advisory Board
   (FOYAB) Foundation Account 110029656 (Account Number: 580.26).

B. The sum of $1,856.22 be added as revenue in the FORPD Miscellaneous Revenue
   Account 97979000.

Passage of the Resolution was moved by Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Director
                           and seconded by Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Vice-
Chair                            .


AYES:
NOES:
ABSTAIN:
ABSENT:
RECUSE:



______________________________                     _____________________________
Delinda Tamagni, Board Chair                       Michael Aho
Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation                    District Administrator


Date: May 21, 2025
            STAFF REPORT

 Meeting Date:        May 21, 2025

 To:                  Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Board of Directors

 From:                Michael Aho – District Administrator

 Subject:             Transfer of Funds from the Fair Oaks Recreation
                      Foundation to the Fair Oaks Recreation and Park District
                      General Fund

 Prepared by:         Jennifer Larkin, Administrative Services Manager

I.     Recommendations
       Approve Resolution No. 052125-04, transferring funds from the Foundation
       Garden Gala Account to the Fair Oaks Recreation and Park District (FORPD)
       Miscellaneous Revenue Account (97979000). (Attachment A)

II.    Background
       In 2023 the District implemented a new event, the Garden Gala for the Arts,
       to both introduce the newly formed Arts and Entertainment division and raise
       funds for arts and entertainment programming and other arts-related needs.

       Initially all expenses for the event were incurred by the District as there were
       no available funds for the first year. Subsequent years have raised enough
       funds to reimburse the District for the costs of the event.

IV.    Problem/ Situation/ Request
       Adoption of Resolution No. 052125-04 will replenish funds for Garden Gala
       expenses initially incurred by the District.

IV.    Financial Analysis
       The attached Foundation Report (Attachment B) shows that the District, over
       the past three fiscal years, has spent $18,326 on Garden Gala expenses. The
       total balance in the Foundation for the Gala is $47,449; the transfer will leave
       $29,123 in the Gala account.
Respectfully Submitted,



Michael Aho
District Administrator

Attachment A:        Resolution 052125-04, to Recognize Revenue from the
                     Garden Gala and to Reflect the Transfer of Funds from the
                     Foundation Account to the Miscellaneous Revenue Account to
                     Offset Expenses Initially Incurred by the District

Attachment B:        Garden Gala Expenditures – Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation
                           RESOLUTION NO. 052125-04

                  BEFORE THE GOVERNING BOARD OF THE
                   FAIR OAKS RECREATION FOUNDATION

                     County of Sacramento, State of California

TO RECOGNIZE REVENUE FROM THE GARDEN GALA AND TO REFLECT
THE TRANSFER OF FUNDS FROM THE FOUNDATION TO THE FAIR OAKS
   RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT MISCELLANEOUS REVENUE
   ACCOUNT TO OFFSET EXPENSES INITIALLY INCURRED BY THE
                         DISTRICT

WHEREAS, The Board of Directors, for the Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation, passed
Resolution No. 052125-04, on May 21, 2025, recognizing the revenues from the Garden
Gala and reflect the transfer of funds from the Foundation Account to the
Miscellaneous Revenue Account to offset expenses initially incurred by the District.

WHEREAS, the procedure to allow the transfer of these now recognized and released
funds requires action by the Board of Directors of the Foundation.

THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED that the Fair Oaks Recreation
Foundation Board of Directors approves the transfer as follows:

A. The sum of $18,326.57 be deducted from the Garden Gala Foundation Account
   110029656 (Account Number: 580.14).

B. The sum of 18,326.57 be added as revenue in the FORPD Miscellaneous Revenue
   Account 97979000.

Passage of the Resolution was moved by Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Director
                           and seconded by Fair Oaks Recreation Foundation Vice-
Chair                            .

NOES:
